subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List - I with List - II:

List-I (Property) List-II (Example)
(a) Diamagnetism (i) MnO
(b) Ferrimagnetism (ii) O2
(c) Paramagnetism (iii) NaCl
(d) Antiferromagnetism (iv) Fe3O4

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List - I with List - II:

List-I (Property) List-II (Example)
(a) Diamagnetism (i) MnO
(b) Ferrimagnetism (ii) O2
(c) Paramagnetism (iii) NaCl
(d) Antiferromagnetism (iv) Fe3O4

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
Antiferromagnetism, type of magnetism in solids in which adjacent ions that behave as tiny magnets

Paramagnetism is a form of magnetism whereby some materials are weakly attracted by an externally applied magnetic field, and form internal, induced magnetic fields in the direction of the applied magnetic field.

MnO- Antiferromagnetism
O2- Paramagnetism
NaCl- Diamagnetism
Fe3O4- Ferrimagnetism

subject: Chemistry
q: Which primitive unit cell has unequal edge lengths <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and all axial angles different from <math><msup><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math>
question: Which primitive unit cell has unequal edge lengths <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and all axial angles different from <math><msup><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math>
solution: The most inorganized system has <math><mi>a</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>c</mi></math> and <math><mi>α</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>90</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> is called the triclinic system.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following statements about packing in solids is incorrect ?
question: Which one of the following statements about packing in solids is incorrect ?
options: Coordination number in bcc mode of packing is 8 .
Coordination number in hep mode of packing is 12 .
Void space in hcp mode of packing is $32 \%$.
Void space in ccp mode of packing is $26 \%$.
solution: The $h c p$ arrangement of atoms occupies $74 \%$ of the available space and thus has $26 \%$ vacant space.

subject: Chemistry
q: All of the following share the same crystal structure except.
question: All of the following share the same crystal structure except.
solution:
$\mathrm{RbCl}, \mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{CsCl}$ share the same crystal structure except $\mathrm{LiCl}$.
$\mathrm{LiCl}$ is deliquescent. It crystalises as a hydrated LiCl. $2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. Other alkali metal chlorides do not form hydrates.

subject: Chemistry
q: Among the following which is the best description of water in the solid phase?
question: Among the following which is the best description of water in the solid phase?
solution: Molecular solid is the best description of water in the solid phase. For example ice is hydrogen bonded molecular solid.

subject: Chemistry
q: In a face centred cubic lattice, atoms of A form the corner points and atoms of B form the face centred points. If two atoms of $\mathrm{A}$ are missing from the corner points, the formula of the ionic compound is :
question: In a face centred cubic lattice, atoms of A form the corner points and atoms of B form the face centred points. If two atoms of $\mathrm{A}$ are missing from the corner points, the formula of the ionic compound is :
solution:
A form corner points and two atoms of A are missing from corner
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad & \text { Atoms at corner }(\mathrm{A})=6 \times \frac{1}{8}=\frac{3}{4} \\
& \text { Atoms at face centre }(\mathrm{B})=6 \times \frac{1}{2}=3 \\
\therefore & \mathrm{A}_{3 / 4} \mathrm{~B}_3 \text { i.e., } \mathrm{AB}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which type of 'defect' has the presence of cations in the interstitial sites?
question: Which type of 'defect' has the presence of cations in the interstitial sites?
solution: In Frenkel defect, some of the ions (usually cation due to their small size) missing from their correct position and occupies interstitial position.

subject: Chemistry
q: Total volume of atoms present in a face-centre cubic unit cell of a metal is ( $r$ is atomic radius)
question: Total volume of atoms present in a face-centre cubic unit cell of a metal is ( $r$ is atomic radius)
solution: $\begin{aligned} V & =n \times\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right) \\ & =4 \times\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\right) \\ & =\frac{16}{3} \pi r^3\end{aligned}$

q: A sample of a metal oxide has formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn></mrow></msub></math>. The metal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> can exist in two oxidation states <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. In the sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the percentage of metal ions existing in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> oxidation state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>. (nearest integer)
question: A sample of a metal oxide has formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn></mrow></msub></math>. The metal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> can exist in two oxidation states <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. In the sample of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>83</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the percentage of metal ions existing in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></math> oxidation state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math>. (nearest integer)
solution:
Let number of ions of M+2=x

Thus, as net charge has to be zero, M+3=0.83-x

⇒2x+3(0.83-x)+1(-2)=0

⇒2x+2.49-3x-2=0

∴x=0.49

%M2+ ions =0.490.83×100=59.03

question:

(Nearest integer): [ Given :NA=6.022×1023 mol-1]
question:
A copper complex crystallising in a CCP lattice with a cell edge of 0.4518 nm has been revealed by employing X-ray diffraction studies. The density of a copper complex is found to be 7.62 g cm-3. The molar mass of copper complex is       gmol-1
(Nearest integer): [ Given :NA=6.022×1023 mol-1]
solution:
dgmcc=4×MNA(a cm)3
7.62=4×M/6.022×10230.4518×10-7 cm3⇒M=105.8 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: A crystal is made up of metal ions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> and oxide ions. Oxide ions form a ccp lattice structure. The cation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> occupies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of octahedral voids and the cation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> occupies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mo>%</mo></math> of tetrahedral voids of oxide lattice. The oxidation numbers of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> are respectively :
question: A crystal is made up of metal ions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> and oxide ions. Oxide ions form a ccp lattice structure. The cation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> occupies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of octahedral voids and the cation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> occupies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mo>%</mo></math> of tetrahedral voids of oxide lattice. The oxidation numbers of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>'</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>'</mo></math> are respectively :
solution:
In the ccp latice of oxide ions effective number of O-2ions =8×18+6×12=4

In the ccp latice,

No. of octahedral voids =4

No. of tetrahedrla voids =8

Given M1 atoms occupies 50% of octahedral voids and M2 atoms occupies 12.5 of tetrahederal voids

No. of M1 metal atoms =4×50100=2

No. of M2 metal atoms =8×12.5100=1

∴ Formula of the compound =M12M2O4

∴ Oxidation states of metals M1 & M2 respectively are +2 and +4

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of number of atoms present in a simple cubic, body centered cubic and face centered cubic structure are, respectively :
question: The ratio of number of atoms present in a simple cubic, body centered cubic and face centered cubic structure are, respectively :
solution:
ZSC = 8 × 18= 1 

[Atoms are only at the corners]

ZBCC = 8 × 18 +1= 1 + 1=2 

[Atoms are at the corners and at the body center]

ZFCC = 8 × 18 + 6 × 12= 1 + 3 = 4  

[Atoms are at the corners and at the face centers]

Here, Z= effective number of atoms in the unit cell.

Therefore, ZSC​: ZBCC​: ZFCC​=1 : 2 : 4

 




SC:BCC:FCC=1:2:4

subject: Chemistry
q: In a face centred cubic lattice, atom A occupies the corner positions and atom B occupies the face centre positions. If one atom of $B$ is missing from one of the face centred points, the formula of the compound is
question: In a face centred cubic lattice, atom A occupies the corner positions and atom B occupies the face centre positions. If one atom of $B$ is missing from one of the face centred points, the formula of the compound is
solution:
Effective no.of $A$ atoms $=\frac{1}{8} \times 8=1$
Effective no.of $B$ atoms $=\frac{1}{2} \times 5$ (One is missing) $=\frac{5}{2}$
Therefore formula is $A_1 B_{\frac{5}{2}}=A_2 B_5$

subject: Chemistry
q: The radius of a calcium ion is $94 \mathrm{pm}$ and of the oxide ion is $146 \mathrm{pm}$. The possible crystal structure of calcium oxide will be
question: The radius of a calcium ion is $94 \mathrm{pm}$ and of the oxide ion is $146 \mathrm{pm}$. The possible crystal structure of calcium oxide will be
solution:
As per formula,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { radius ratio } & =\frac{\text { radius of cation }}{\text { radius of anion }} \\
& =\frac{94}{146}=0.643
\end{aligned}
$$
Since the value is between $0.414-0.732$ hence the coordination no. will be 6 and geometry will be octahedral.

question: In a monoclinic unit cell, the relation of sides and angles are respectively:
solution: No Solution Available

subject: Chemistry
q: The incorrect statement about the imperfections in solids is
question: The incorrect statement about the imperfections in solids is
options: Schottky defect decreases the density of the substance.
Frenkel defect does not alter the density of the substance.
Interstitial defect increases the density of the substance.
Vacancy defect increases the density of the substance.
solution:
Frenkel Defect: This defect is shown by ionic solids. The smaller ion (usually cation) is dislocated from its normal site to an interstitial site. It creates a vacancy defect at its original site and an interstitial defect at its new location. Frenkel defect is also called dislocation defect. It does not change the density of the solid. In all other defects there is a change in the density.

In Schottky defect equal number of cation and anion are missing from their position so, it decreases the density of the substance.

In Interstitial defect extra atoms are present at interstitial position so, it increases the density of the substance.

Due to vacancy defect density of the substance will decrease. since atoms are missing from their positions.

q: The empirical formula for a compound with a cubic close packed arrangement of anions and with cations occupying all the octahedral sites in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Integer answer)
question: The empirical formula for a compound with a cubic close packed arrangement of anions and with cations occupying all the octahedral sites in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Integer answer)
solution:
In AxB
Effective number of B atoms =4 (in CCP)
Effective number of A atoms =4 (all O.V.)
So formula of the compound =A4B4=AB
So x=1

subject: Chemistry
q: A cubic solid is made up of two elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>. Atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> are present on every alternate corner and one at the center of cube. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>rd</mi></msup></math> of the total faces. The empirical formula of the compound is
question: A cubic solid is made up of two elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>. Atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> are present on every alternate corner and one at the center of cube. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>rd</mi></msup></math> of the total faces. The empirical formula of the compound is
solution:
The share of the atom present at corner of the cube is 1/8. The share of the atom at face centre is 1/2. Hence, the molecule can be represented as follows,

X4×18+1×1Y6×13×12

⇒X12+1  Y1

⇒X23  Y1

⇒X1.5  Y1(Empirical formula)

⇒X3  Y2.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Metal M crystallizes into a FCC lattice with the edge length of 4.0×10-8 cm. The atomic mass of the metal is g/mol.

(Use : NA=6.02×1023 mol-1, density of metal, M=9.03 g cm-3)
question:
Metal M crystallizes into a FCC lattice with the edge length of 4.0×10-8 cm. The atomic mass of the metal is g/mol.

(Use : NA=6.02×1023 mol-1, density of metal, M=9.03 g cm-3)
solution:
Given ,a=4×10-8 cm

d=9.03 g/ml

d=ZMNAa3

Volume of a unit cell = a3, where a is the edge length

d is the density of the solid substance and M is the molar mass.

Mass of the unit cell = number of atoms in unit cell × mass of each atom =Z×m

(Here z is the number of atoms present in one unit cell and m is the mass of a single atom)M=9.03×6.02×1023×64×10-244=86.97g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: An element having an atomic radius of $0.14 \mathrm{~nm}$ crystallizes in an $f_{c c}$ unit cell. What is the length of a side of the cell ?
question: An element having an atomic radius of $0.14 \mathrm{~nm}$ crystallizes in an $f_{c c}$ unit cell. What is the length of a side of the cell ?
solution:
For a fcc unit cell
$\begin{aligned}
& r=\frac{\sqrt{2} a}{4} \\
& a=\frac{4 r}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \sqrt{2} \times 0.14=0.39 \approx 0.4 \mathrm{~nm}.
\end{aligned}$

question: Percentage of free space in cubic close packed structure and in body centred packed structure are respectively

subject: Chemistry
q:
The distance between Na+ and Cl- ions in solid NaCl of density 43.1 gcm-3 is .....×10-10 m. (Nearest Integer)
(Given : NA=6.02×1023 mol-1)
question:
The distance between Na+ and Cl- ions in solid NaCl of density 43.1 gcm-3 is .....×10-10 m. (Nearest Integer)
(Given : NA=6.02×1023 mol-1)
solution:
For NaCl Z=4 and M=58.5 gram

d=Z×MNA×Volume

43.1=4×58.56.02×1023×a3

a3=4×58.56.02×43.1×10-23

=0.9×10-23

=9×10-24

a=2.08×10-8 cm

dNa++Cl-=a2=2.08×10-102 m ≃1×10-10 m

subject: Chemistry
q: A metal M crystallizes into two lattices :- face centred cubic (fcc) and body centred cubic (bcc) with unit cell edge length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>°</mo></mover></math> respectively. The ratio of densities of lattices fcc to bec for the metal M is _______(Nearest integer)
question: A metal M crystallizes into two lattices :- face centred cubic (fcc) and body centred cubic (bcc) with unit cell edge length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>°</mo></mover></math> respectively. The ratio of densities of lattices fcc to bec for the metal M is _______(Nearest integer)
solution:
The density of the crystal lattice can be calculated as follows,

d=Z×MNAa3

Z=effective number of atoms in unit cell(For BCC=2 and FCC=4)

M=Molar mass

NA=Avogadro's number

a=Edge length

dFCCdBCC=4×MwNA×(2)32×MwNA×(2.5)3=3.90

This solid is most likely to be a / an :

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ga</mi></math> (atomic mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>) crystallizes in a hexagonal close packed structure. The total number of voids in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>581</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ga</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ga</mi></math> (atomic mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>) crystallizes in a hexagonal close packed structure. The total number of voids in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>581</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ga</mi></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
HCP structure : Per atom, there will be one octahedral void (OV) and two tetrahedral voids (TV).

Therefore, total three voids per atom are present in HCP structure.

→ Therefore total no. of atoms of Ga will be-

= Mass  Molar Mass ×NA=0.581 g70 g/mol×6.023×1023

→ Now, total Number of voids =3 × total no. of atoms 

=3×0.58170×6.023×1023=14.99×1021

≃15×1021

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A: Sharp glass edge becomes smooth on heating it up to its melting point.

Reason R: The viscosity of glass decreases on melting.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as
Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A: Sharp glass edge becomes smooth on heating it up to its melting point.

Reason R: The viscosity of glass decreases on melting.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is false

Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct
explanation of A.
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is true.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution: Sharp glass edges are heated to make them smooth. Because on heating glass melts and the surface of the liquid tends to take the rounded shape at the edges which has minimum surface area. This is called fire polishing of glass. Correct explanation for this is surface tension.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following arrangements shows the schematic alignment of magnetic moments of antiferromagnetic substance?
question: Which of the following arrangements shows the schematic alignment of magnetic moments of antiferromagnetic substance?
solution: When the magnetic moments align in a regular pattern with neighbouring spins pointing in opposite directions (as in d), the substance shows antiferromagnetism.

subject: Chemistry
q: An element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> crystallises in a body centred cubic unit cell with a cell edge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi>pm</mi></math>. The density of the element is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The number of atoms present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the element is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: An element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> crystallises in a body centred cubic unit cell with a cell edge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi>pm</mi></math>. The density of the element is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The number of atoms present in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the element is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
M is body centred cubic, ∴ Z=2

Let mass of 1 atom of M is A

Edge length =300pm

Density =6 g/cm3

∴ 6 g/cm3=Z×A300×10-103=2×A27×10-24

A=81×10-24 g

Atoms of M=Total massMass of one atom = 18081×10-24 = 22.22×1023≅22×1023

subject: Chemistry
q: Two elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> which form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> type compounds. If both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> weigh equally, then the atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is____times of atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>.
question: Two elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> which form <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> type compounds. If both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AB</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> weigh equally, then the atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is____times of atomic weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>.
solution:
Given: Molar mass of A2B=AB3

∴ 2A+B=A+3BA→Atomicwt. of AB→Atomicwt. of B

⇒A=2 B

∴ atomic wt. of A is 2 times of atomic wt. of B Integer answer is 2.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math> <mrow> <mtext>CsCl</mtext></mrow> </math> crystallises in body centred cubic lattice. If 'a' is its edge length then which of the following expressions is correct?
question: <math> <mrow> <mtext>CsCl</mtext></mrow> </math> crystallises in body centred cubic lattice. If 'a' is its edge length then which of the following expressions is correct?
solution:
8 chloride ions at 8 corners Cs + cation at body centre along body diagonal there will be one Cs + cation and 2  Cl − ions at ends of body diagonal.
2 r Cs + + 2 r Cl - = 3 a
a is edge length

subject: Chemistry
q: Ionic radii of cation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> and anion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>102</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>181</mn><mi>pm</mi></math> respectively. These ions are allowed to crystallize into an ionic solid. This crystal has cubic close packing for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo></msup><mo>.</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> is present in all octahedral voids. The edge length of the unit cell of the crystal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AB</mi></math> is___<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pm</mi></math>.
question: Ionic radii of cation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> and anion <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>102</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>181</mn><mi>pm</mi></math> respectively. These ions are allowed to crystallize into an ionic solid. This crystal has cubic close packing for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>-</mo></msup><mo>.</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> is present in all octahedral voids. The edge length of the unit cell of the crystal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AB</mi></math> is___<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pm</mi></math>.
solution:
In given ionic compound anions form CCP lattice, And cation is present in octahedral void. Means this structure is similar to structure of NaCl. Which is also called rock salt structure.

Edge length = a=?

In rock salt structure cations and anions are in contact along the edge of the cube. 

So, a=2r++r-

a=2102+181

a=2283

a=566 pm
